insights.headline: Group bookings are the highest cancellation risk at 61.1%
insights.evidence: Groups: 61.1% cancellation rate (n=19,810). Direct: 15.3%. Corporate: 18.7%. Groups cancel at 4x the rate of the lowest-risk segment.
insights.limitation: High cancellation rate does not automatically mean low profitability — groups may have higher ADR or volume that offsets cancellations. Revenue impact needs separate analysis.
insights.action: Implement non-refundable deposit requirements for group bookings and require binding group coordinator agreements with confirmed headcount 14 days before arrival.

insights.headline: Long lead time bookings cancel at 68% — the longer the advance, the higher the risk
insights.evidence: 365d+ bookings: 68.0% cancel. 180-364d: 58.1%. 90-179d: 47.8%. 0-2d: 8.1%. Rate increases monotonically across every lead time bucket.
insights.limitation: Correlation does not prove causation — customers booking far ahead may be inherently more uncertain. Cannot determine if this is behaviour or booking system driven.
insights.action: Require higher deposits for bookings >90 days out. Implement automated re-confirmation campaigns at 60, 30, and 14 days before arrival to reduce passive cancellations.

insights.headline: OTA channel cancels at 2.3x the rate of direct bookings at equal ADR
insights.evidence: TA/TO channel: 41.0% cancel vs Direct: 17.5%. ADR nearly identical: €117.96 (OTA) vs €117.69 (Direct). Equal pricing for 2.3x higher cancellation risk.
insights.limitation: TA/TO channels may drive higher volume or different demographics that justify higher cancellation. Lifetime value and repeat booking rates were not analysed.
insights.action: Renegotiate OTA contracts to include cancellation penalties or non-refundable deposit requirements. Shift marketing budget to direct channel.

insights.headline: City hotels cancel 13-16 percentage points more than resort hotels
insights.evidence: City Hotel properties: 40.6-43.2% cancellation rate. Resort Hotel properties: 26.3-29.9%. Gap is consistent across all locations — structural, not geographic.
insights.limitation: City vs resort differences may reflect different customer segments (business vs leisure) rather than property practices. Dataset is not aparthotel-specific.
insights.action: Investigate why resort hotels retain more bookings. Apply successful retention practices (deposit policies, pre-arrival engagement) to city portfolio.

insights.headline: Special requests signal committed guests — r=−0.235 with cancellation
insights.evidence: Special requests: r=−0.235 with is_canceled. Parking spaces: r=−0.195. Lead time: r=+0.293 (strongest positive predictor). Engaged guests cancel significantly less.
insights.limitation: r=−0.235 is a moderate correlation. Guests making requests may simply be more organised — the act of requesting may not itself reduce cancellation intent.
insights.action: Flag zero-request bookings combined with long lead time and OTA channel as highest-risk profile. Send pre-arrival preference surveys to increase engagement signals.

insights.headline: Near-flat seasonality: only €5.42 ADR gap despite 20.9% volume swing
insights.evidence: Peak ADR: October €118.43. Low ADR: May €113.00. Gap: €5.42. But booking volume swings 20.9% between peak and low months.
insights.limitation: This dataset covers traditional hotels, not aparthotels specifically. Aparthotels may have even flatter seasonality due to corporate extended-stay demand.
insights.action: Implement dynamic pricing — the chain has almost zero rate variation despite significant demand variation. Even modest peak-period rate increases would materially improve RevPAR.

insights.headline: Weekend stays priced lower than weekday stays — pricing premium is missing
insights.evidence: Weekend-heavy stays: €115.50 mean ADR. Weekday-heavy stays: €115.85. Weekdays are actually priced higher despite lower leisure demand.
insights.limitation: Dataset may mix business travel (weekday) and leisure (weekend) in ways that explain the difference. Needs segmentation by customer_type to confirm.
insights.action: Add a weekend surcharge for leisure market segments. Friday-Sunday bookings from leisure guests are less price-sensitive and should command a premium.

insights.headline: Corporate segment is most reliable but lowest-priced — underpriced relative to value
insights.evidence: Corporate ADR: €114.36 — lowest of all segments. Corporate cancel rate: 18.7% — second lowest. Most reliable segment being charged the least.
insights.limitation: Corporate rates are often negotiated via contracts — raising them unilaterally could lose key accounts. Needs contract review before any pricing change.
insights.action: Review corporate rate contracts at next renewal. Even a €5-10 ADR increase on the most reliable segment improves revenue predictability without cancellation risk.

insights.headline: 93.8% of guests have no parking booked — massive pre-arrival upsell pool
insights.evidence: 93.8% of bookings have required_car_parking_spaces = 0. Parking-requesting guests also cancel less (r=−0.195). Pre-arrival parking offer costs nothing to automate.
insights.limitation: Not all properties have parking. The offer should only be sent for properties with available parking capacity.
insights.action: Add parking upsell to every pre-arrival guest communication for applicable properties. Automate via n8n webhook triggered 72h before check-in.

insights.headline: Repeat guests cancel at half the rate but pay 28% less — loyalty is underpriced
insights.evidence: Repeat guests: 16.2% cancel rate, €75 mean ADR. First-time guests: 38.1% cancel rate, €104 mean ADR. Repeat guests are the most valuable segment but pay least.
insights.limitation: Low repeat guest rate (2.8%) means sample size is small (n=163). Patterns may not be fully representative.
insights.action: Build a loyalty programme that incentivises direct repeat bookings at fair market rates. Target the OTA-converted guests who are most likely to book direct on return.
